facts.text: The Atari 2600 used a MOS 6507 CPU — a cost-reduced 6502 running at 1.19 MHz with a narrower address bus.
Combat was the pack-in game for the Atari 2600 at launch in 1977, offering 27 game variations in a single cartridge.
Pitfall! on the Atari 2600 sold over 4 million copies and was one of the first true action-adventure games on a home console.
The Atari 2600 joystick used a standard 9-pin DE-9 connector that was later adopted by Sega, Commodore, and many others.
E.T. the Extra-Terrestrial cartridges were famously buried in a New Mexico landfill in 1983 — a discovery confirmed by excavation in 2014.
The original PlayStation was codenamed 'Play Station' during its development as a SNES CD-ROM add-on for Nintendo.
Crash Bandicoot used a streaming technique to load levels without visible load screens, which was revolutionary for PS1.
The PlayStation DualShock controller introduced both rumble feedback and dual analog sticks in 1997.
Tomb Raider's Lara Croft was originally conceived as a male archaeologist before the team switched to a female lead.
The original PlayStation sold over 102 million units worldwide, making it Sony's most successful first-generation console.
The Atari Jaguar was marketed as 64-bit but actually combined a 16-bit and two 32-bit processors.
Only about 67 games were commercially released for the Atari Jaguar during its entire lifespan.
Alien vs. Predator for the Jaguar was praised as one of the best first-person shooters of 1994.
The Jaguar CD add-on launched in 1995 but had fewer than 15 games released for it before being discontinued.
The Atari Jaguar controller had 17 buttons including a numeric keypad, making it one of the most complex of its era.
Super Mario Bros. 3 was secretly revealed to Western audiences in the 1989 film 'The Wizard' before its North American release.
The NES lockout chip (10NES) required licensed cartridges to pass authentication before play would begin.
Mega Man 2 was nearly not greenlit by Capcom — the first game sold poorly, so the sequel was created as an internal passion project.
The Konami Code (Up Up Down Down Left Right Left Right B A) was first used in Gradius on NES to grant the player power-ups.
EarthBound (Mother 2) on SNES had aggressive anti-piracy code that caused the game to crash near the final boss if a copied cart was detected.
Star Fox used the Super FX chip — a custom graphics co-processor built into the cartridge — to render 3D polygons on SNES hardware.
Donkey Kong Country used pre-rendered 3D graphics that gave it a look unlike anything else on 16-bit hardware.
The SNES version of Mortal Kombat replaced blood with grey sweat and altered fatalities, while the Genesis version kept them via a code.
Secret of Mana's ring-based menu system could be opened mid-action, letting players configure spells without a pause screen.
Chrono Trigger featured 13 different endings depending on when and how the player defeated the final boss.
Sonic the Hedgehog's speed was engineered specifically to showcase the Sega Genesis CPU's power over the SNES.
The Sega Genesis had a built-in Yamaha FM synthesis sound chip, giving it a distinctly different audio character from the SNES's SPC700.
Sonic & Knuckles used 'lock-on technology' to physically combine with Sonic 2 or 3 cartridges for expanded content.
The Genesis launched in North America in 1989 bundled with Altered Beast and a six-button controller wasn't standard until 1993.
NBA Jam on Genesis contained hidden unlockable characters including Bill Clinton and the Beastie Boys.
The Nintendo 64 was the last major cartridge-based home console before the industry completed its shift to optical discs.
GoldenEye 007 was developed by a team with almost no prior game development experience, many of whom had never shipped a title.
Ocarina of Time's Z-targeting system was so influential it became the standard for 3D action-adventure games for decades.
The N64 controller's center prong was designed for 3D games while the left prong was intended for 2D titles — players rarely used both at once.
Conker's Bad Fur Day on N64 was initially designed as a family platformer before being completely redesigned as an adult-oriented satire.
Shenmue was the most expensive video game ever made at the time of its 1999 release, costing an estimated $70 million.
Seaman for the Dreamcast used the microphone peripheral and voice recognition to let players hold conversations with a fish with a human face.
Jet Set Radio was one of the first games to use cel-shading to create a stylized animated look in 3D.
The Dreamcast VMU had its own screen, speaker, and D-pad, functioning as a standalone mini-console and memory card.
Power Stone on Dreamcast pioneered the arena brawler genre, influencing games like Super Smash Bros. Melee.
The PS2's Emotion Engine CPU was briefly reviewed by the U.S. government to see if it qualified as restricted military-grade technology.
Shadow of the Colossus featured only 16 enemies across its entire world, each one a full environmental puzzle.
Guitar Hero launched on PS2 in 2005 and popularized the plastic instrument peripheral genre that dominated the late 2000s.
Ico is widely credited as one of the first games to convincingly portray an AI companion the player genuinely cared about.
The PS2 remained in production until 2013 — 13 years after its launch — the longest production run of any PlayStation console.
Panzer Dragoon Saga is one of the rarest Saturn games, with only 6,000 copies printed in North America.
The Saturn's 2D hardware was significantly more capable than the PlayStation's, making it the preferred platform for 2D fighters.
Guardian Heroes on Saturn featured a complex branching story and battles with dozens of enemies on screen simultaneously.
Radiant Silvergun on Saturn is still regarded as one of the greatest shoot-em-ups ever made and commands high prices for original copies.
Dragon Force on Saturn featured battles with up to 100 soldiers fighting simultaneously on screen.
Neo Geo cartridges could hold up to 330 MB of data — many times more than competing home consoles of the early 1990s.
The Neo Geo AES home console used identical hardware to SNK's MVS arcade cabinets, making ports truly arcade-perfect.
Fatal Fury introduced a two-plane fighting system where characters could dodge between a foreground and background lane.
The Neo Geo CD launched in 1994 to make Neo Geo games more affordable, but long disc load times frustrated players.
The Neo Geo MVS arcade board could hold up to six different game cartridges in a single cabinet, reducing operator costs.
The Sega Game Gear required six AA batteries and lasted only 3-5 hours, compared to the Game Boy's 15-30 hours on four AAs.
Sega released a TV tuner accessory for the Game Gear, allowing the handheld to function as a portable television.
The Game Gear had a Master Gear Converter accessory that allowed players to use Sega Master System cartridges.
Many Game Gear titles were ports of Master System games due to the nearly identical hardware architectures of the two systems.
The Game Gear featured a backlit color screen from day one, which the Game Boy didn't achieve until the Game Boy Light in 1998.
The Game Boy Advance was fully backward compatible with original Game Boy and Game Boy Color cartridges.
Mother 3 was released for GBA in Japan in 2006 but was never officially localized for Western markets despite fan demand.
The GBA SP introduced a clamshell design and a front-lit screen — later revisions added a true backlight.
Castlevania: Aria of Sorrow introduced the soul system, letting players absorb enemy abilities as power-ups.
The Game Boy Advance sold over 81 million units worldwide, making it one of Nintendo's most successful handhelds.
The PSP Go was the first major handheld console to eliminate physical media entirely in favor of digital downloads.
Crisis Core: Final Fantasy VII on PSP served as a prequel to the PS1 classic and was critically acclaimed worldwide.
The PSP featured a widescreen 16:9 display, unusual for handhelds at its 2005 launch.
Lumines was the PSP's killer app at launch — a music-based puzzle game designed by Rez creator Tetsuya Mizuguchi.
Monster Hunter became a cultural phenomenon in Japan on PSP, with players gathering in public spots to hunt together via local wireless.
The 3DO launched in 1993 at $699, making it the most expensive console at launch up to that point.
The 3DO's hardware was licensed to multiple manufacturers — Panasonic, Sanyo, and Goldstar all made their own versions.
Road Rash on 3DO featured full-motion video cutscenes and was considered a showcase title for the system.
The 3DO was the first home console to use CD-ROM as its primary media format from launch.
Despite its advanced hardware, the 3DO failed commercially due to its high price and competition from the PlayStation and Saturn.
The Commodore 64 is the best-selling single computer model of all time, with an estimated 12-17 million units sold.
The C64's SID sound chip was so musically capable it sparked an entire demoscene chiptune culture that continues today.
Many future game developers cut their teeth on the C64, including founders of Bullfrog, DMA Design, and Rare.
The C64 could display 16 colors with hardware sprites, making it notably better for games than many contemporary home computers.
Elite on the C64 used procedural generation to create a galaxy of 256 star systems within a tiny memory footprint.
The Mattel Intellivision was marketed as the first 16-bit home console, though its CPU was technically a 16-bit chip running in a specialized configuration.
The Intellivision's numeric keypad controller used game-specific overlays to label buttons differently for each title.
The Intellivision was the first system to offer downloadable games via the PlayCable cable TV service in 1981.
Intellivision sports games were used in head-to-head TV advertisements against Atari 2600 versions to highlight the graphical difference.
The Intellivision II was a redesigned cheaper model released in 1983 that had minor compatibility issues with some original games.
The ColecoVision launched with Donkey Kong as a pack-in game, delivering a near-perfect arcade port that wowed consumers.
The ColecoVision Expansion Module #1 allowed it to play Atari 2600 cartridges, which led to a lawsuit from Atari.
The ColecoVision used the same Zilog Z80 processor as the Sega Master System, released years later.
Zaxxon on ColecoVision was one of the first home ports of an isometric arcade game and showcased the system's power.
Coleco planned an ADAM computer add-on for the ColecoVision, but its troubled launch contributed to the 1983 video game crash.
The Vectrex was the only home console to use vector graphics, projecting lines directly onto its built-in CRT screen.
Every Vectrex unit shipped with Minestorm built in — the only console to include a pack-in game on the hardware itself.
Vectrex games used color plastic overlays placed over the screen to add color to otherwise black-and-white vector graphics.
The Vectrex had a 3D imager accessory that used a spinning disc to create an early form of stereoscopic 3D gaming.
After GCE was acquired by Milton Bradley, the Vectrex was discontinued following the 1983 crash despite being critically praised.
The Magnavox Odyssey, released in 1972, was the first home video game console ever sold commercially.
The original Odyssey used no microprocessor — it was built entirely from transistors, diodes, and capacitors.
Odyssey games used translucent plastic overlays placed on the TV screen since the console could not generate its own graphics.
Ralph Baer, the inventor of the Magnavox Odyssey, is widely recognized as the 'Father of Video Games.'
Atari settled a lawsuit with Magnavox after Pong was found to infringe on the Odyssey's table tennis game patent.
The TurboGrafx-16 was actually an 8-bit system with a 16-bit graphics processor — its '16' referred to the GPU, not the CPU.
The PC Engine CD-ROM² was the first CD-ROM add-on for a game console, released in Japan in 1988.
The PC Engine (TurboGrafx-16) was the top-selling console in Japan before the Super Famicom launched.
Bonk's Adventure served as the TurboGrafx-16's mascot game, featuring a cave boy who headbutts everything in his path.
The TurboGrafx-16 had a TurboTap accessory allowing up to five players to connect simultaneously for multiplayer games.
The Sega Master System outsold the NES in Europe and Brazil, where it remained commercially active into the 2000s.
The Master System had both a cartridge slot and a card slot for smaller, cheaper Sega MyCard game releases.
Brazilian manufacturer Tectoy produced Master System hardware and software for decades, releasing new games well into the 2010s.
Alex Kidd in Miracle World was built into many Master System consoles as a pack-in game, bootable without a cartridge.
The Sega Master System used a Zilog Z80 CPU — the same chip found in the ColecoVision and ZX Spectrum.
Night Trap, a full-motion video game on Sega CD, was cited by name during 1993 U.S. Congressional hearings on video game violence.
Sonic CD introduced time travel as a core mechanic and is considered by many to have the best soundtrack in the Sonic series.
Snatcher, Hideo Kojima's cyberpunk visual novel, was released on Sega CD and is now extremely rare and valuable.
The Sega CD could play standard audio CDs through the Genesis's headphone jack, making it a dual-purpose entertainment device.
The Sega CD featured hardware scaling and rotation effects similar to the SNES Mode 7 chip.
The 32X was designed as a stopgap measure to extend the Genesis's life while Sega finished developing the Saturn.
The 32X added two 32-bit Hitachi SH-2 processors to the Genesis, inserted via the cartridge slot.
Only 40 games were ever released for the Sega 32X during its extremely short commercial lifespan.
Doom on the 32X was a marquee title but was considered inferior to the PC version due to missing levels and music.
The 32X Combo with a Sega CD created the 32X CD, the rarest of the three configurations with only six games released.
The GameCube used proprietary miniDVD discs holding 1.5 GB of data, primarily to deter piracy.
Metroid Prime was developed by Retro Studios — a then-unproven team — and is now considered one of the greatest games ever made.
Resident Evil 4 was originally planned for PS2 but launched as a GameCube exclusive before being ported months later.
The GameCube had a built-in carrying handle, making it the most literally portable home console Nintendo had released.
The GameCube controller's oversized A button was deliberately designed as the default action button for 3D platformers.
The Game Boy Color could display 56 simultaneous colors from a palette of 32,768 possible shades.
The GBC was backward compatible with all original Game Boy games, which were displayed in a limited color palette chosen by the player.
Pokemon Gold and Silver pushed the GBC hardware to its absolute limits, famously squeezing in all of Kanto as a post-game bonus region.
The Game Boy Color included an infrared port used for short-range wireless communication in select games.
Dragon Warrior Monsters on GBC was a surprise hit that launched a long-running monster-collecting spinoff series.
The Atari Lynx was the first handheld gaming device with a backlit color LCD screen, released in 1989.
The Lynx featured hardware sprite scaling and distortion in silicon, enabling smooth zooming effects rare for handheld hardware.
The Lynx could be flipped upside-down for left-handed play, with a software flag telling the game to mirror the display.
The ComLynx cable allowed up to 17 Lynx units to connect for multiplayer, far exceeding what the Game Boy's Link Cable supported.
The Lynx's backlit color screen required six AA batteries and gave only around 4 hours of play per set.
The WonderSwan was designed by Gunpei Yokoi, creator of the original Game Boy, after he left Nintendo following the Virtual Boy's failure.
The WonderSwan could be held both vertically and horizontally, with face buttons on both orientations to support either layout.
The WonderSwan Color released in 2000 could display 241 simultaneous colors from a palette of 4,096.
Final Fantasy I through VI were all remade exclusively for the WonderSwan Color in Japan, never leaving the platform.
The WonderSwan ran on a single AA battery and boasted over 30 hours of battery life — far outlasting its competitors.
The Neo Geo Pocket was SNK's first handheld console, released in Japan in 1998 in monochrome before a color version followed.
The Neo Geo Pocket Color's microswitched thumbstick had a satisfying clicky feel praised by players and critics alike.
SNK vs. Capcom: Match of the Millennium on NGPC is widely considered one of the best handheld fighting games ever made.
The Neo Geo Pocket Color could link with the Dreamcast to exchange save data and unlock bonus content in select games.
The NGPC library was heavily focused on SNK's fighting franchises, with strong entries from King of Fighters, Samurai Shodown, and Fatal Fury.
The Virtual Boy was Nintendo's first 32-bit console and used red LED arrays to create stereoscopic 3D visuals.
The Virtual Boy required players to hold their face against a fixed visor — it could not be used while moving or in bright light.
Only 22 games were officially released for the Virtual Boy in North America before Nintendo discontinued it in 1996.
Wario Land was considered the Virtual Boy's strongest title — one of the few games that genuinely used the 3D depth creatively.
Gunpei Yokoi reportedly did not want to release the Virtual Boy when it shipped, feeling the technology wasn't ready.
The Nintendo DS was codenamed 'Project Nitro' during its internal development at Nintendo.
New Super Mario Bros. was the first new 2D Mario platformer in over a decade when it launched on DS in 2006.
The DS microphone was used creatively in Nintendogs, where players could call their dog by speaking its name aloud.
The Nintendo DS sold over 154 million units, making it the second best-selling dedicated game system of all time.
Brain Age on DS became a surprise phenomenon, expanding the DS audience to older players who had never owned a game system.
The 3DS StreetPass feature automatically exchanged console data with nearby 3DS units even when the system was in sleep mode.
Fire Emblem Awakening saved the Fire Emblem franchise from cancellation — Nintendo had planned it as the series' final entry.
The New Nintendo 3DS added a C-stick nub and extra ZL/ZR shoulder buttons for games requiring more input options.
The 3DS featured a built-in pedometer that counted steps and rewarded players with Play Coins usable in various games.
Xenoblade Chronicles 3D was exclusive to the New Nintendo 3DS because standard 3DS hardware lacked the processing power to run it.
The original Xbox was the first console to ship with a built-in hard drive, eliminating the need for memory cards.
Halo: Combat Evolved was designed as a launch title to showcase the Xbox and largely defined the console first-person shooter genre.
Xbox Live launched in November 2002, establishing the blueprint for modern subscription-based online console gaming.
The original Xbox controller — nicknamed 'The Duke' — was criticized for being too large for many players' hands, leading to a smaller 'Controller S' revision.
The Xbox used a modified Windows 2000 kernel as its operating system, which made it relatively straightforward for PC developers to port games.
The Wii Remote used a combination of accelerometers and an infrared sensor bar for its motion-based input.
Wii Sports was included with the Wii in North America and became the best-selling Wii game of all time.
The Wii outsold both the PS3 and Xbox 360 by a wide margin, driven by its appeal to non-traditional gamers.
The Wii had hidden GameCube controller ports under a cover on top, allowing it to play the entire GameCube library.
Wii Fit and its balance board peripheral sold over 22 million units, making it one of the best-selling gaming peripherals ever.
The Atari 5200's analog joystick did not self-center, making it frustrating for many action games that required precise inputs.
The Atari 5200 was incompatible with Atari 2600 games without a separate adapter accessory sold separately.
The Atari 5200 was the first Atari console to feature a pause button on its controller.
The Atari 5200's hardware was essentially identical to the Atari 400/800 home computer lineup.
Pole Position was one of the Atari 5200's most popular titles and featured an impressive driving simulation for 1983.
The Atari 7800 was backward compatible with the Atari 2600 library — one of the first consoles to support backward compatibility.
The 7800 was originally ready for release in 1984 but was shelved when Warner Bros. sold Atari, delaying launch until 1986.
The Atari 7800 used a cartridge format with no proprietary lockout chip, making unlicensed development straightforward.
Commando was one of the most praised arcade ports on the Atari 7800 for its faithful recreation of the cabinet experience.
The 7800 ProLine joystick was a significant improvement over the 5200 controller, featuring a self-centering design.
The PS3's Cell processor was a joint development between Sony, Toshiba, and IBM, designed as a general-purpose supercomputer chip.
The PS3's Blu-ray drive helped the format decisively win the HD disc format war against Toshiba's HD-DVD.
Stanford University's Folding@home project ran on PS3, making the combined network one of the most powerful distributed computing systems ever assembled.
The PS3 launched at $599 in the US, making it one of the most expensive console launches in gaming history.
LittleBigPlanet pioneered user-generated content on consoles at mass scale, letting players build and share entire game levels.
The Wii U was Nintendo's first console to support HD graphics output, a feature competitors had offered since 2005-2006.
The Wii U GamePad's touchscreen enabled off-TV play, letting players enjoy games without using a television at all.
Splatoon debuted on the Wii U in 2015, marking Nintendo's first successful new major IP in many years.
The Wii U suffered from widespread consumer confusion — many buyers believed the GamePad was simply a Wii accessory.
Super Smash Bros. for Wii U supported 8-player simultaneous battles on large stages, the most players in the series' history.
The Sega Pico was a educational console aimed at young children that used a touchpad and special storybook cartridges.
The Atari ST used Motorola's 68000 CPU and rivaled the Amiga as a serious gaming and music production platform in Europe.
The Nintendo Switch is the first console to be a genuine hybrid — fully functional as both a home console and a portable handheld.
The Sega SC-3000 was a home computer released alongside the SG-1000 in Japan in 1983, sharing identical internals.
The NEC PC-FX was the last cartridge-free 32-bit console to launch before Sony and Sega dominated the market.
The Fairchild Channel F was the first cartridge-based home video game console, predating the Atari 2600 by a year.
The Emerson Arcadia 2001 was one of many consoles rushed to market to capitalize on the Atari 2600's success, with a library of 35 games.
The Bally Astrocade had a built-in version of Gunfight and used a unique combined joystick-and-trigger controller.
The Entex Adventure Vision used a single column of LEDs reflected by a spinning mirror to simulate a display — one of the most unusual screen technologies in gaming history.
The SG-1000 was Sega's first home console, released on the exact same day as the Famicom in Japan on July 15, 1983.
